text: This video is to demonstrate robotic assisted radical parametrectomy with pelvic lymph node dissection in the case of CA cervix by Dr. Rohit Raghunath Ranade and his team.

Here is a forty-six years old multiparous woman with previous two caesarean section. She had history of heavy menstrual bleeding, which was evaluated and treated elsewhere. The pre-operative ultrasound was suggestive of cervical fibroid, hence she had androgon, a total laparoscopic hysterectomy with bilateral salving oophorectomy one month back. Post-operative histopath was suggested for adenocarcinoma of cervix without ectocervical inflammation. Clinical examination was insignificant and the post-op CT showed no evidence of pelvic mass lesion or collection in the pelvis. No lymphadenopathy.
Hence, she was planned for robotic assisted radical parametrectomy with pelvic lymphoma dissection.
The surgeon initiates the procedure by placing robotic ports in the abdominal wall, preparing for robotic-assisted radical parametrectomy and pelvic lymph node dissection.
The surgeon places the primary port in the supraumbilical region using Hasen's method, preparing for robotic-assisted radical parametrectomy.
After placing the ports, the robot is done.
Here we can see the post-operative changes.
Adhesions between the vaginal vault and the sigmoid colon are carefully released using monopolar scissors.
The surgeon releases adhesions between the sigmoid colon and the left lateral pelvic wall using monopolar scissors, focusing on the area between the mis-entry and the lateral pelvic wall.
The surgeon opens the right retroperitoneal space, creating pelvic spaces as part of the procedure.
The surgeon identifies the iliac vessel and the ureter within the surgical field, highlighting their anatomical positions relative to surrounding structures.
The surgeon continues the dissection to create the paravesical and pararectal spaces, focusing on the lateral paravesical and lateral pararectal areas.
The surgeon visualizes the right uterine artery after creating the pelvic spaces. The uterine artery is traced to its origin and skeletonized, revealing its course and surrounding structures.
The surgeon ligates the uterine artery using a ligar clip, followed by cauterization and cutting of the artery.
The developed pelvic spaces, including the lateral parava cycle space and lateral pararectal space, are visible, with clear delineation of anatomical structures such as the iliac vessel and ureter.

The lateral parava cycle space is shown, bounded laterally by the parietal pelvic fascia, external iliac vessel, and retroinguinal space; medially by the obliterated umbilical artery; cranially by the lateral parametrium with the uterine artery and vein; caudally by the retro-inguinal space; ventrally by the round ligament and the anterior leaf of the broad ligament; and dorsally by the pelvic floor.

The Lutzko's lateral pararectal space is shown, with its boundaries defined by the parietal pelvic fascia, internal iliac artery, and birefomous muscle laterally; the ureter and mesourator with the hypogastric nerve medially; the pre-sacral fascia and sacral bone cranially; the lateral parametrium containing the deep uterine vein and pelvic splenic nerve caudally; the peritoneal layer over the ureter, uterine artery, and superficial uterine vein ventrally; and the pelvic floor dorsally.

The surgeon identifies the medial paravesical space, which is visually bounded laterally by the obliterated umbilical artery and the lateral paravesical space, medially by the umbilical pre-vesical fascia and paracorpus, cranially by the lateral parametrium, caudally by the bladder wall, ventrally by the round ligament, and dorsally by the pelvic floor.
The surgeon clamps the right infundibulopelvic ligament using a ligatril, then cauterizes and cuts the ligament, completing the ligation process.
The surgeon dissects the ureter further up to the ureteric tunnel. The medial pararectal space is created, and the dissection is extended downwards towards the rectum.
The surgeon creates the pre-rectal space, manipulating tissue layers to establish a clear anatomical area in front of the rectum.
The surgeon cauterizes and cuts the round ligament, completing the separation of this structure.
The surgeon dissects the anterior leaf of the broad ligament, continuing the dissection towards the bladder from the lateral aspect.
The surgeon visualizes the medial paravisical space and the lateral parametrium, revealing the anatomical structures in the surgical field.
The surgeon repeats the dissection steps on the left side, including visualizing the medial paravisical space, dissecting the anterior leaf of the brown ligament, and creating the pre-rectal space.
The surgeon opens the left retroperitoneal space, identifying the ureter and iliac vessels within the surgical field.
The surgeon creates spaces on the left side, mirroring the dissection on the right. The uterine artery is traced visually up to its origin.
The surgeon ligates the uterine artery with a niger clip, followed by cauterization and cutting of the artery.

The surgeon visualizes the pelvic spaces on the left side, including the medial paravisical space and lateral parametrium, with previously identified structures such as the ureter and iliac vessels visible.
The surgeon places a ligar clip on the left infundibulopelvic ligament, then cauterizes and cuts the ligament, completing the dissection in the left pelvic space.

The surgeon dissects the peritoneal layer and infundibulopelvic ligament downwards after creating the left medial pararectal space, extending the dissection to the level of the rectum and connecting it to the prerectal space.
The surgeon dissects the anterior leaf of the left broad ligament and proceeds with bladder dissection from the lateral aspect on the left side. The round ligament is cauterized and cut.
Parametrium is an anatomical structure consisting of the connective tissue lying between the parietal pelvic fascia and the visceral pelvic fascia.
The lateral parametrium, also known as the paracervix, is visible after the development of the paravesical and pararectal spaces.

The surgeon initiates bladder dissection from the lateral aspect using a Mecarni tube as a vaginal probe. The bladder peritoneum is identified and incised, followed by downward dissection of the bladder inferiorly to the level of the middle third of the vagina.

The surgeon completes the dissection of the bladder pillar, joining the vesico-vaginal space to the paravesical space, effectively separating the bladder from the anterior vaginal wall. The ureter is dissected in preparation for the upcoming parametrectomy.

The surgeon mobilizes the ureters from their attachment, separating them from the medial leaf of the peritoneum. The dissection continues down to the ureteric tunnel, extending up to the ureters' entry into the bladder.
The surgeon excises the lateral parametrium after isolating the ureter, followed by an upper vaginectomy where the vagina is excised with an adequate margin.

On the right side, the surgeon dissects the bladder pillar and joins the paravaginal and vesicovaginal spaces. The ureter is isolated with precision, using an additional energy source, a liver shell, through the assistance port to facilitate the dissection.
The surgeon excises the lateral parametrium and proceeds with the vaginectomy, joining the previous incision to remove the specimen.
The surgeon delivers the excised specimen vaginally, placing it inside an incarnate tube. Following this, the radical pelvic lymphoma dissection is initiated.

The surgeon performs bilateral lymph node dissection from the bifurcation of the common iliac artery cranially to the circumflex iliac vessel and the cupous ligament caudally. The dissection margins are defined by the external iliac vessels laterally and the umbilical artery medially. Lymph nodes in the obturator space are removed and subsequently delivered vaginally.
The surgeon closes the vaginal vault using a 3-0 V-lock suture. Hemostasis is checked and confirmed. The robotic system is then unlocked, and the port sites are closed.
the postoperative irreversible. The postoperative histopath was suggestive of no residual disease with negative lymph node. Hence, she is planned for regular follow-up now.

I would like to conclude that the radical parametrectomy presents with lower complications, making it a preferred approach to treat younger patients with early-stage invasive carcinoma cervix with a prior history of simple histrectomy when compared to radiation therapy. The minimally invasive procedures like the robotic-assisted surgery is feasible and effective than the traditional laparotomy for performing the radical parametretin. Thank you.
